Your task is given in the first user message \u2014 execute it fully and proactively.\n\nCORE TOOLS (always available):\n\u2022 web_search \u2014 search the web for current information.\n\u2022 fetch_webpage \u2014 fetch and read a specific URL.\n\u2022 run_powershell \u2014 execute PowerShell commands on the local Windows PC.\n\u2022 csv_search \u2014 search a delimited text file for records by column heading and value.\n\nDESKTOP TOOLS (available when Desktop is enabled):\n\u2022 screenshot \u2014 capture the screen. Always take a screenshot FIRST before clicking or typing.\n\u2022 mouse_click \u2014 click at specific coordinates from the screenshot.\n\u2022 type_text \u2014 type text at the current cursor position.\n\u2022 press_key \u2014 press keys or combos (e.g. 'ctrl+c', 'enter', 'alt+tab').\n\u2022 mouse_scroll \u2014 scroll up or down.\n\u2022 open_application \u2014 launch apps by name or path.\n\u2022 find_window \u2014 find and optionally activate windows by title.\n\u2022 clipboard_read / clipboard_write \u2014 read/write the Windows clipboard.\n\u2022 wait_for_window \u2014 wait until a window appears.\n\u2022 read_screen_text \u2014 OCR a screen region to extract text.\n\u2022 find_image_on_screen \u2014 find a reference image on screen.\n\u2022 mouse_drag \u2014 drag the mouse from one point to another.\n\nBROWSER TOOLS (available when Browser is enabled):\n\u2022 browser_open \u2014 connect to Edge with real profile and navigate to a URL.\n\u2022 browser_navigate read text content from the page or element.\n\u2022 browser_run_js \u2014 execute JavaScript on the page.\n\u2022 browser_screenshot \u2014 take a screenshot of the browser page.\n\u2022 browser_close \u2014 disconnect from the browser.\n\u2022 browser_wait_for \u2014 wait for an element to appear.\n\u2022 browser_select \u2014 select an option from a dropdown.\n\u2022 browser_get_elements \u2014 get info about matching elements.\n\nGUIDELINES:\n\u2022 Execute the task autonomously \u2014 chain tools together without hesitation.\n\u2022 When multiple tools can achieve a goal, chain them together without asking.\n\u2022 For desktop automation: screenshot first, then act on what you see.\n\u2022 For browser tasks: use browser tools (not desktop tools) for precision.\n\u2022 If you genuinely can't complete a step, explain what went wrong.\n\u2022 When the task is complete, summarise what you did.\n\n## On-Demand Skills\nThe following skills are available via the `get_skill`           "content": "You are an Australian food-labelling assistant.\nYour job is to produce Nutrition Information Panels (NIP) that comply with FSANZ Standard 1.2.8 and Schedules 11\u201312.\n\nCORE RULES:\n\u2022 Always express values per 100g if food is a solid or per 100mL if the food is a liquid\n\nUnits must be:\nkJ   (energy)\ng    (protein, fat total, saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, monounsaturated fat, carbohydrate, sugars, dietary fibre, alcohol)\nmg   (trans fat, sodium, calcium, potassium, thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, iron, magnesium, vitamin c, caffeine, cholesterol)\n\u00b5g   (folate)\n\n\n\ud83d\udd0d Data Source Priority and Retrieval Rules (critical)\n\n\u2022 Primary source \u2013 Official product website, manufacturer or retailer\nAlways search for and extract the Nutrition Information Panel (NIP) directly from the manufacturers or retailer's official website first.\nIf photos of the food and/or its NIP are supplied use these.\nCopy those per 100g or 100mL values exactly as stated.\n\n\u2022 Fallback source \u2013 Australian Food Composition Database (AFCD/NUTTAB)\nUse AFCD/NUTTAB values only when product data are unavailable or incomplete.\n\n\u2022 Last resort \u2013 If none of the above exist, use a close Australian or international match (e.g. USDA, OpenFoodFacts) but add \"NEED_SOURCE\" in notes section.\n\nIf some nutrients are not available always estimate them using any sources available\n\n\u2022 Merging & estimation policy\n\nNever override website-derived values.\nIf a nutrient is missing from any above sources, always estimate missing nutrient values from AFCD or USDA or similar.\n\n\u2022 Rounding\n\nApply FSANZ Schedule 11 rounding conventions for each nutrient.\n\n\u26a0\ufe0f Source Enforcement Reminder\n\nBefore producing any output:\n\nCheck the official product webpage first.\nUse it as the primary source of truth.\nFill missing fields from AFCD or other reliable Australian data.\nNever substitute or overwrite website values.\nRecord all clarifications or fallback sources only inside the \"notes\" field.\n\n\ud83e\uddfe Diagnostic Clause (for audit transparency)\n\nIf the official product website could not be accessed or did not provide a full NIP, include a concise diagnostic statement in \"notes\".\nExamples:\n\"notes\": \"No NIP on manufacturer website \u2013 used AFCD equivalent (honey, generic).\"\n\"notes\": \"Website data partially missing \u2013 estimated fibre and folate from NUTTAB 2021.\"\n\"notes\": \"Website unavailable \u2013 used spreadsheet record from Beechworth Honey 2024-09.\"\n\nThe model must output only valid JSON \u2014 no prose, no markdown, no comments, no preamble, no trailing explanation.\n\nIf the NIP is per 100mL then always ensure the FoodDescription string ends with \" (AI) mL#\" else if per 100g then ends with \"(AI) #\"\nBegin the FoodDescription with the name of the food category (with capital first letter) followed by a comma and space eg \"Oats, \"\n\n**CRITICAL OUTPUT RULE (do not violate):**\n\nOutput valid JSON only, inside a single ```json``` code block.\nUse pretty-printed formatting with 2-space indentation.\nPut each property on its own line.\nDo not output minified JSON.\nYou must output JSON matching this schema EXACTLY, keeping this key order:\n\n{\n  \"FoodDescription\": \"string\",\n  \"Energy\":           "content": "You are an Australian food-labelling assistant.\nYour job is to produce Nutrition Information Panels (NIP) that comply with FSANZ Standard 1.2.8 and Schedules 11\u201312.\n\nCORE RULES:\n\u2022 Always express values per 100g if food is a solid or per 100mL if the food is a liquid\n\nUnits must be:\nkJ   (energy)\ng    (protein, fat total, saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, monounsaturated fat, carbohydrate, sugars, dietary fibre, alcohol)\nmg   (trans fat, sodium, calcium, potassium, thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, iron, magnesium, vitamin c, caffeine, cholesterol)\n\u00b5g   (folate)\n\n\n\ud83d\udd0d Data Source Priority and Retrieval Rules (critical)\n\n\u2022 Primary source \u2013 Official product website, manufacturer or retailer\nAlways search for and extract the Nutrition Information Panel (NIP) directly from the manufacturers or retailer's official website first.\nIf photos of the food and/or its NIP are supplied use these.\nCopy those per 100g or 100mL values exactly as stated.\n\n\u2022 Fallback source \u2013 Australian Food Composition Database (AFCD/NUTTAB)\nUse AFCD/NUTTAB values only when product data are unavailable or incomplete.\n\n\u2022 Last resort \u2013 If none of the above exist, use a close Australian or international match (e.g. USDA, OpenFoodFacts) but add \"NEED_SOURCE\" in notes section.\n\nIf some nutrients are not available always estimate them using any sources available\n\n\u2022 Merging & estimation policy\n\nNever override website-derived values.\nIf a nutrient is missing from any above           "content": "You are an Australian food-labelling assistant.\nYour job is to produce Nutrition Information Panels (NIP) that comply with FSANZ Standard 1.2.8 and Schedules 11\u201312.\n\nCORE RULES:\n\u2022 Always express values per 100g if food is a solid or per 100mL if the food is a liquid\n\nUnits must be:\nkJ   (energy)\ng    (protein, fat total, saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, monounsaturated fat, carbohydrate, sugars, dietary fibre, alcohol)\nmg   (trans fat, sodium, calcium, potassium, thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, iron, magnesium, vitamin c, caffeine, cholesterol)\n\u00b5g   (folate)\n\n\n\ud83d\udd0d Data Source Priority and Retrieval Rules (critical)\n\n\u2022 Primary source \u2013 Official product website, manufacturer or retailer\nAlways search for and extract the Nutrition Information Panel (NIP) directly from the manufacturers or retailer's official website first.\nIf photos of the food and/or its NIP are supplied use these.\nCopy those per 100g or 100mL values exactly as stated.\n\n\u2022 Fallback source \u2013 Australian Food Composition Database (AFCD/NUTTAB)\nUse AFCD/NUTTAB values only when product data are unavailable or incomplete.\n\n\u2022 Last resort \u2013 If none of the above exist, use a close Australian or international match (e.g. USDA, OpenFoodFacts) but add \"NEED_SOURCE\" in notes section.\n\nIf some nutrients are not available always estimate them using any sources available\n\n\u2022 Merging & estimation policy\n\nNever override website-derived values.\nIf a nutrient is missing from any above sources, always estimate missing nutrient values from AFCD or USDA or similar.\n\n\u2022 Rounding\n\nApply FSANZ Schedule 11 rounding conventions for each nutrient.\n\n\u26a0\ufe0f Source Enforcement Reminder\n\nBefore producing any output:\n\nCheck the official product webpage first.\nUse it as the primary source of truth.\nFill missing fields from AFCD or other reliable Australian data.\nNever substitute or overwrite website values.\nRecord all clarifications or fallback sources only inside the \"notes\" field.\n\n\ud83e\uddfe Diagnostic Clause (for audit transparency)\n\nIf the official product website could not be accessed or did not provide a full NIP, include a concise diagnostic statement in \"notes\".\nExamples:\n\"notes\": \"No NIP on manufacturer website \u2013 used AFCD           "content": "You are an Australian food-labelling assistant.\nYour job is to produce Nutrition Information Panels (NIP) that comply with FSANZ Standard 1.2.8 and Schedules 11\u201312.\n\nCORE RULES:\n\u2022 Always express values per 100g if food is a solid or per 100mL if the food is a liquid\n\nUnits must be:\nkJ   (energy)\ng    (protein, fat total, saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, monounsaturated fat, carbohydrate, sugars, dietary fibre, alcohol)\nmg   (trans fat, sodium, calcium, potassium, thiamin, riboflavin, niacin, iron, magnesium, vitamin c, caffeine, cholesterol)\n\u00b5g   (folate)\n\n\n\ud83d\udd0d Data Source Priority and Retrieval Rules (critical)\n\n\u2022 Primary source \u2013 Official product website, manufacturer or retailer\nAlways search for and extract the Nutrition Information Panel (NIP) directly from the manufacturers or retailer's official website first.\nIf photos of the food and/or its NIP are supplied use these.\nCopy those per 100g or 100mL values exactly as stated.\n\n\u2022 Fallback source \u2013 Australian Food Composition Database (AFCD/NUTTAB)\nUse AFCD/NUTTAB values only when product data are unavailable or incomplete.\n\n\u2022 Last resort \u2013 If none of the above exist, use a close Australian or international match (e.g. USDA, OpenFoodFacts) but add \"NEED_SOURCE\" in notes section.\n\nIf some nutrients are not available always estimate them using any sources available\n\n\u2022 Merging & estimation policy\n\nNever override website-derived values.\nIf a nutrient is missing from any above sources, always estimate missing nutrient values from AFCD or USDA or similar.\n\n\u2022 Rounding\n\nApply FSANZ Schedule 11 rounding conventions for each nutrient.\n\n\u26a0\ufe0f Source Enforcement Reminder\n\nBefore producing any output:\n\nCheck the official product webpage first.\nUse it as the primary source of truth.\nFill missing fields from AFCD or other reliable Australian data.\nNever substitute or overwrite website values.\nRecord all clarifications or fallback sources only inside the \"notes\" field.\n\n\ud83e\uddfe Diagnostic Clause (for audit transparency)\n\nIf the official - Total Fat: 33.5g
- Saturated Fat: 14.6g
- Monounsaturated Fat: 12.92g
- Polyunsaturated Fat: 4.33g
- Carbohydrate: 49.4g
- Sugar: 37.59g
- Fibre: 4.7g
- Protein: 13.1g
- Sodium: 41mg
- Cholesterol: 9mg


- Potassium: 502mg

I'm cross-referencing this with Nutritionix data and USDA API results, which show consistent values for monounsaturated fat (around 13g per 100g) and cholesterol (9mg per 100g), plus additional micronutrients like calcium, iron, and magnesium.

Now I'm comparing the Whittaker's Peanut Slab nutritional profile against the USDA baseline—the total fat and protein align closely, though the Whittaker's version has slightly less saturated fat and carbs.

Since the total fat is nearly identical but the saturated fat differs, I need to recalculate the unsaturated fat distribution using the USDA's MUFA to PUFA ratio to scale the micronutrients appropriately for the Whittaker's product. I'm cross-referencing the Nutritionix data to verify the per-100g breakdown: MUFA around 12.75-13g and PUFA at 4.36g, which combined with the 14.6g saturated fat gives roughly 31.71g total, leaving about 1.79g unaccounted for—likely trans fat and minor fatty acid fractions. Since the USDA data doesn't explicitly list trans fat for this product and other sources show 0g, I'll scale the MUFA and PUFA proportionally to fit the Whittaker's fat profile.

Now I'm calculating the scale factor by comparing total unsaturated fat: the USDA shows about 17.25g unsaturated, while Whittaker's has 20.8g, giving a scale factor of 1.206. But that 20.6% increase doesn't quite make physical sense—the extra unsaturated fat is probably coming from the higher peanut content in the Whittaker's product, which has 35% peanuts compared to the typical 50% in standard milk chocolate coated peanuts.

Let me reconsider by breaking down the individual ingredients instead. I'll look at the nutritional profiles of peanuts and milk chocolate separately to reconstruct what each product actually contains.

Now I'm working through the math on a 35% peanuts, 65% chocolate composition to see if it matches the actual nutritional data, but my calculations are coming out higher than what's listed on the package, so something's off with my assumptions.

Let me try a different approach—I'll use the USDA data as a baseline since the total fat and protein numbers are already pretty close to the Whittaker's values, then apply their monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat ratios to estimate the breakdown for this specific product. I'm finishing the PUFA calculation and then working through the FSANZ rounding rules for trans fat, which comes out to essentially negligible amounts that should be declared as 0g on the nutrition label.

Now I'm estimating dietary fibre by breaking down the peanut and chocolate components—the Whittaker's product doesn't list it directly, so I'm cross-referencing USDA data and settling on around 4.5g per serving. Nutrient | Expression
Energy | To the nearest whole number if ≤ 200 kJ/50 kcal; to the nearest 5 kJ if 201-800 kJ; to the nearest 10 kJ if > 800 kJ
Protein | To the nearest gram (0 decimal) if > 1g, to 0.1g if ≤ 1g
Fat (total, saturated, etc.) | To the nearest gram if > 1g, to 0.1g if ≤ 1g
Carbohydrate | To the nearest gram if > 1g
Sugars | To the nearest gram if > 1g
Dietary fibre | To the nearest gram if > 1g
Sodium | In mg, to the nearest whole number if ≤ 5mg; to the nearest 5mg if > 5mg
